# Buffer Overflow Exploitation: A Complete Walkthrough

## Why Buffer Overflows Are Dangerous

Buffer overflows are critical security vulnerabilities because they allow attackers to **execute arbitrary code** with the same privileges as the vulnerable program. This happens when a program writes more data to a buffer than it can hold, overwriting adjacent memory locations including return addresses

To explain buffer overflows to students, it is best to frame it as a **violation of the program's control flow**. The danger isn't just that a program crashes (Denial of Service), but that an attacker can trick the CPU into executing instructions that the original programmer never wrote.
